claim: Self-correcting agents follow a reusable three-step pattern: diagnose the situation, generate a solution, execute the solution; this works for any failure mode with concrete outputs
actionable: Implement a diagnosis-generation-execution loop for error correction in any system producing concrete results (SQL, code, queries); reuse this pattern across projects

claim: Tight feedback loops (short distance between action and iteration) are the hallmark of great developers; LLMs enable auto-correction to tighten these loops dramatically
actionable: Design systems to minimize latency between error detection and correction; use LLMs to automate the correction step to compress iteration cycles

claim: Assistants API with GPT-4 Turbo enables long-term memory (threads), file uploads with RAG, and tool calling; this combination is powerful but expensive and complex to scale
actionable: Use Assistants API when you need persistent context across multiple turns AND file-based knowledge AND tool calling; otherwise replicate with simpler multi-turn LLM calls

claim: Database rollback on failed transactions is critical before running correction agents; otherwise correction attempts execute against a locked transaction state
actionable: In database-connected agents, always rollback failed transactions before running self-correction loops to reset state

claim: Uploading table schema definitions as files to Assistants API enables effective SQL error diagnosis; agents can reference schema context when troubleshooting query failures
actionable: For SQL-generating agents, upload schema definitions as files to Assistants API to provide structured context for error diagnosis

claim: Assistants API is overkill for most use cases; three sequential prompts with tool calls often achieve the same result at lower cost and lower latency
actionable: Before using Assistants API, try implementing multi-step prompts with explicit tool calls; likely simpler and cheaper for non-stateful workflows

claim: Assistants API scales poorly at high throughput due to per-user agent creation limits and API call frequency; sustainability is questionable for applications with thousands of concurrent users
actionable: For consumer-scale applications (1000+ concurrent users), avoid per-user Assistants API agents; use pooled agents or stateless prompt calls instead

claim: Depend on OpenAI API uptime as a critical risk; API outages block applications completely; require backup strategies for production agentic systems
actionable: For production systems using Assistants API, implement fallback modes, error messaging for API downtime, and consider hybrid solutions with local/alternative models

claim: LLMs auto-correct unanticipated errors well; agents can discover and fix problems you didn't predict, providing value beyond the original design intent
actionable: Build safety nets with LLM-based error correction even when you can't enumerate all possible failures; the models often find unexpected solutions

claim: Detailed logging of agent reasoning (chat files, intermediate messages) is essential for debugging and iterating; without it, failure modes are opaque
actionable: For any agentic system, implement structured logging of all agent reasoning steps; save intermediate prompts and responses for post-hoc analysis
